findings.topic: Reddit account strategy — volume vs longevity
findings.title: Run more accounts at lower post frequency instead of spamming one account
findings.claim: Posting 5 posts/account/day across 10+ accounts outlasts and outperforms blasting 20 posts/day from one account, because account longevity compounds karma and trust.
findings.what_they_do: Each account posts ~5 times per day max in its first month. Agency runs 10–20+ accounts simultaneously across different subreddits and niches. Total output (e.g. 50 posts/day from 10 accounts) is high, but no single account looks like a spam bot. Accounts survive weeks-to-months instead of 48 hours, building karma that unlocks higher-trust subreddits over time.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit traffic strategy (§5 GAP, §8 build-toward). No internal baseline exists for account count targets, post frequency, or subreddit selection.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a 'survival speed first' Reddit SOP: cap new accounts at 5 posts/day for the first 30 days, run a minimum of 10 accounts per creator/model, and track account lifespan as the primary health KPI rather than daily post volume. Build this into the Traffic & Distribution gap (§5).

sources.quote: we post less per account and we run more accounts. For example, we might do five posts per account per day. That's not low. That's enough. And if you run 10 accounts, that's 50 posts a day total.

findings.topic: Reddit account segmentation by niche and subreddit
findings.title: Assign each Reddit account a focused niche and unique subreddit set to avoid mod detection
findings.claim: Posting the same model from multiple accounts in the same subreddit triggers mod bans; spreading accounts across different niches and subreddits mimics organic behavior and extends account life.
findings.what_they_do: Each account is scoped to a specific niche angle and a non-overlapping set of subreddits. No two accounts for the same model post in the same subreddit. Profile content is kept internally consistent so it reads as a real person posting, not an agency dump. This both protects accounts and improves conversion because profiles look authentic.
findings.vs_us: No internal Reddit segmentation strategy exists at Palm (§5 GAP). Palm's AI persona work (§3) does maintain per-persona account isolation on IG, which is an analogous principle — that isolation logic should extend to Reddit accounts.
findings.recommendation: When building the Reddit SOP, enforce a one-subreddit-set-per-account rule with a master spreadsheet mapping (account → subreddit list → niche angle). Mirror the per-persona isolation logic already used in AI account setup (§3) to Reddit account management.

sources.quote: Every account is focused, not a random, not posting everywhere, focused. Each account posts in different subreddits and usually in a slightly different niche.

findings.title: Warm Reddit accounts with passive lurking and off-niche karma before any promo posting
findings.claim: Skipping the warm-up phase is the primary reason accounts get banned; new accounts must look like real users before any promotional or explicit content is introduced.
findings.what_they_do: Day 1: scroll only, no posts, no links, no bio. Day 2: one or two organic comments in non-adult subreddits (games, hobbies, pets). Then: text posts and normal comments in general subs to build karma. Profile picture and bio added later. Links and OF CTAs added last, only after account has demonstrated normal user behavior. Karma is built in smaller, engagement-friendly subs rather than large subreddits full of bot behavior.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature 90-day AI account warmup cadence for Instagram (§4), but no equivalent for Reddit accounts. The IG warmup logic (§4) is structurally similar and could be adapted. Real-creator Reddit warming is also undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Draft a Reddit-specific warmup SOP modeled on Palm's existing IG warmup cadence (§4). Key milestones: Day 1–2 passive only; Day 3–7 organic comments in hobby subs; Day 8–14 normal text posts; Day 15–21 profile picture + bio; Day 22+ profile link and soft promo. Assign warmup tasks to a VA role, mirroring the Warmup Tasks table structure already in Airtable (§4).

sources.quote: Warming is basically acting like a normal user before you start posting. Day one, scroll and read stuff. Don't post. Don't add links. Don't try to look like a marketer.

findings.topic: Reddit device strategy — phones vs antidetect browsers
findings.title: Use old iPhones for Reddit accounts to get higher trust scores and fewer bans
findings.claim: Reddit's trust scoring treats mobile (especially iPhone) users as more legitimate than desktop/proxy setups, making used iPhones a cost-effective way to reduce ban rates.
findings.what_they_do: Agency runs many Reddit accounts on used iPhone 8s and iPhone XSs purchased cheaply. Condition of the phone is irrelevant (cracked screens fine). The alternative — antidetect browser + proxies on desktop — is cheaper and easier to outsource but carries higher ban risk if proxy quality is poor. Trade-off framed as: desktop = cheaper/scalable/riskier; phones = more expensive/harder to manage/longer-lived accounts.
findings.vs_us: Palm already uses hardware-isolated GrapheneOS Pixel phones for AI persona IG accounts (§3), with 1 SIM per persona. The same hardware-isolation philosophy could extend to Reddit. However, the agency here uses iPhones specifically for Reddit trust, not Pixels — a nuance worth noting.
findings.recommendation: Evaluate adding a small fleet of used iPhones (not Pixels) specifically for Reddit account management, separate from the AI-persona Pixel fleet. Start with 5–10 devices. Track account lifespan vs desktop/proxy setup to validate the trust-score claim empirically before scaling. Document in SIM Inventory and device tracking tables already in Airtable (§3).

sources.quote: Phones have higher trust. Reddit trust mobile users more, especially iPhones. They're harder to spoof, so Reddit treats them like real humans. We run a lot of accounts on used iPhone 8s and iPhone XSs.

findings.title: Route Reddit traffic through a landing page or Telegram before the OF link to protect funnel from bans
findings.claim: Sending Reddit traffic directly to an OF subscription link risks both link flagging and total audience loss when accounts are banned; a landing page or Telegram buffer preserves the audience.
findings.what_they_do: Reddit posts link to a landing page or Telegram channel first. The OF subscription link is one step deeper in the funnel. When a Reddit account is banned, the agency retains the audience already in Telegram or on the landing page and can redirect them without rebuilding from zero. Direct OF links are avoided on Reddit because the platform flags them.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit funnel (§5 GAP). Palm does use Beacons as a link-in-bio layer for AI personas (§3), which is structurally similar to the landing page buffer concept. The Telegram infrastructure already exists at Palm for internal coordination (§5) and could potentially be repurposed as a fan-facing buffer layer.
findings.recommendation: When building the Reddit traffic SOP, mandate a two-step funnel: Reddit post → landing page (Beacons or equivalent) → OF link. Explore whether Palm's existing Telegram infrastructure could serve as the fan-audience retention layer for Reddit traffic, insulating the OF funnel from account bans. Document this in the Traffic & Distribution section (§5 GAP).

sources.quote: we usually send traffic to a landing page or Telegram first, then from there to a subscription link. That way, if Reddit kills an account, you don't lose your whole audience.

findings.title: Modify content files before reposting to avoid Reddit's hash-based reuse detection
findings.claim: Reddit detects reposted content at the file level; reusing the exact same file from a banned account will get the new account flagged regardless of which subreddit it's posted in.
findings.what_they_do: Agency explicitly warns against reposting the same exact file. When repurposing content across accounts or after a ban, files must be modified (specific techniques kept inside their private community). The simple rule stated publicly: never upload the same unmodified file twice. This is treated as one of the top causes of cascade bans.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline produces assets stored in Airtable (§1, §2), but there is no documented Reddit content-modification step before posting. The social pipeline (§1) focuses on IG/TikTok formats and does not include a Reddit-specific asset preparation stage.
findings.recommendation: Add a Reddit asset preparation step to the content pipeline (§1) that applies file-level modifications (metadata strip, re-encode, crop variation, or similar) before any asset is posted to Reddit. This should be a checklist item in the editor workflow or a separate VA task, distinct from the main editing queue. Treat the specific modification techniques as a gap to research further.

sources.quote: Reddit recognizes reused content way more than people realize. If an account gets banned and you repost the same exact file somewhere else, Reddit can detect it and you get flagged again.

findings.title: Split Reddit ops into two dedicated VA roles: one for warming/karma, one for posting
findings.claim: Scaling Reddit requires separating the warming and karma-building function from the daily posting function into distinct VA roles, and automation tools must be avoided entirely.
findings.what_they_do: At scale, the agency uses one VA focused on account warming and karma building (must write naturally, not like a robot — robotic writing is flagged) and a separate VA for daily posting. Automation tools are explicitly prohibited because Reddit detects and bans automated behavior faster than human VAs. Human VAs beat software on Reddit in both account longevity and detection avoidance.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting on IG/TikTok via Telegram routing (§5) and has editor roles defined in Clerk (§10). There is no Reddit-specific VA role or SOP. Palm's general stance on automation (Publer for IG) would not translate to Reddit given this agency's warning.
findings.recommendation: When staffing Reddit operations, define two distinct VA roles in Palm's org structure (§10 GAP): a 'Reddit Warmer' responsible for account aging and karma, and a 'Reddit Poster' for daily content uploads. Explicitly exclude Reddit from any Publer or automation tooling. Add Reddit VA hiring criteria and natural-writing quality checks to the hiring process (§10 GAP).

sources.quote: A VA for warming and karma. A VA for posting. Don't use automation tools. Reddit hates automation. You'll get cooked. Also, human VAs beat software on Reddit every time.

findings.title: Define and track sub-to-message revenue ratios (1:2 trash → 1:15 elite)
findings.claim: The single most important chatting KPI is the ratio of subscription revenue to message revenue, and most agencies operate at a trash-tier 1:2–1:3 when elite is 1:8–1:15+.
findings.what_they_do: Griffin defines three tiers: trash = 1:1–1:3, good = 1:5–1:7, elite = 1:8–1:15+. The agency shoots for 1:8–1:15 sustained over 6 months. Ratio is calculated by dividing total message revenue by total subscription revenue. It is tracked per chatter and per account, reviewed at every end-of-shift report, and used as the primary performance lever for both chatter coaching and firing decisions.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8). We have no documented chatting-ratio benchmark, no internal tracking of sub-to-message revenue split, and no SOP for using this metric as a chatter KPI. Whale-hunting data exists (§9) but is not linked to a ratio framework.
findings.recommendation: Adopt the chatting-ratio framework as the core metric for evaluating the external chat team today and as the foundational KPI when building the in-house chat team. Instrument Airtable or the admin portal to auto-compute sub revenue vs. message revenue per creator per week. Set a contractual floor (e.g. 1:5 minimum) in the external team SLA. Target 1:8+ as the internal team's launch benchmark.

sources.quote: trash is 1 to 1:3... good is going to be a 1:5 to 1:7... elite, which is where I shoot for, is anything from 1 to 8 to 1 to 15 plus

findings.title: Segment every subscriber into four buyer tiers and tailor spend effort accordingly
findings.claim: Subscribers fall into four distinct buyer types—freebie hunters (70%), casual buyers (20%), custom buyers (8%), whales (2%)—and treating all fans the same destroys revenue.
findings.what_they_do: Freebie hunters: test with $3–5 micro-PPV; if no buy, drop them. Casual buyers: send PPVs 2–3× per week, rotate content types, price $10–15 teasers / $25–35 longer cuts. Custom buyers: offer personalized customs fast at $50–$300 depending on length. Whales: assign to only 1–2 designated chatters, build deep parasocial relationship, offer GFE/video calls/exclusive access, never ghost. Each segment gets a different script approach and time investment.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) with Fan Tracker CRM and going-cold detection, so whale identification has a partial internal baseline. However there is no documented four-tier segmentation framework, no micro-PPV testing protocol for freebie hunters, and no written casual-buyer PPV cadence. The external chat team likely applies its own segmentation invisibly to Palm.
findings.recommendation: Codify the four-tier framework in Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9). Add a 'buyer tier' field to fan records. Build a triage SOP: on welcome, classify within the first 2 interactions and route to the appropriate funnel. Share the framework with the external chat team as a required operating standard. Pre-build this data model in Airtable ahead of the in-house chat team launch.

sources.quote: there is four types of people that will subscribe to your page... freebie hunters... casual buyers... custom buyers... whales
your whales only will really make up 2% of your entire clientele... but they can be responsible for 30% of revenue

findings.topic: Welcome sequence and fan intel gathering
findings.title: Run a 3-step timed welcome sequence to open every new sub and build a fan profile
findings.claim: A structured welcome sequence—timed first message, incentivized fallback, and intel-gathering conversation—sets up every subsequent sale and is what 99% of agencies skip.
findings.what_they_do: Step 1: wait 90–120 seconds after sub, send a one-question opener (e.g. 'OMG thanks for the sub, what's your name?'). Step 2: if no reply after 2 minutes, send an automated incentive message offering a free pick for responding now, with urgency framing. Step 3: if still no reply, send a content teaser image asking 'is this what you came for?'; if that fails, move on. Step 4 (intel): when in conversation, collect name, job, payday, kinks/preferences, personality, and purchase history; log everything in OF fan notes so every future chatter can read it before engaging.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal welcome SOP exists. The fan intel/profile concept partially maps to Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) but there is no documented fan-notes standard or welcome-sequence protocol. This is a core build-toward gap for the in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: Document this exact 3-step welcome sequence as the first SOP for the in-house chat team. Create a standardized fan-profile template (name, job, payday, kinks, personality, purchase history, last contact date) and map it to fields in the Fan Tracker Airtable table. Share the template with the external chat team immediately as a minimum standard. Build an automation (e.g. via Airtable + Make) to prompt chatters to complete the profile within the first 3 interactions.

sources.quote: step one of this is a welcome message... we wait a minute and a half to two minutes and send them this message
gather intel is step two... collect their real name, their job, their payday, their kinks and preferences, their personality

findings.title: Use a tiered PPV pricing ladder: $10–15 teasers, $25–35 standard, $50–300 customs
findings.claim: Pricing PPVs without a defined ladder leaves money on the table; a tiered structure from micro-PPV to premium custom anchors fan spend and prevents both under- and over-pricing.
findings.what_they_do: Micro-PPV for tire kickers: $3–5. Regular PPV teasers: $10–15. Longer standard PPVs: $25–35. Custom short video (2–4 min): $50–75+. Custom long/personalized: $100–300. Subscription page: $10 with 50% discount landing at $5 (preferred over 90% discount). Customs are offered only after intel is gathered and personalized to the fan's stated kinks. Objection handling script: 'My nudes are expensive, I don't want to hurt your pockets.'
findings.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model is documented at Palm (§7, marked #1 creator concern and biggest gap). This is a direct fill for that gap. The external chat team applies its own pricing logic invisibly.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this ladder as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Document it in a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team as a required standard. Test the $10/50%-discount sub page price against current sub pricing on at least one creator. When building the in-house chat team, bake the pricing ladder into chatter training materials alongside the objection-handling script.

sources.quote: pricing structure, 10 to 15 for a teaser, 25 to 35 longer
a $10 thing with a 50% discount... flat at $5... I find this more beneficial than running a 90% discount
minimum price is $50 for a very short video... moving up to $200, $300 for the big full-time videos and personalized talk

findings.title: Assign whales to 1–2 designated chatters only; build parasocial relationship to retain $10K+/mo spenders
findings.claim: Whales (2% of fans, up to 30% of revenue) require dedicated chatter assignment, rapid response, free content gifts, and deliberate parasocial relationship-building to retain long-term.
findings.what_they_do: Identify whales by: buying PPV within 24h of sub, tipping unprompted, sending long personal messages. Mark fan notes 'DO NOT CHAT' for general chatters; assign only 1–2 named chatters. Reply within 10–30 minutes (24/7 coverage for whales). Use fan's name constantly, ask about their life, send free content occasionally, never charge for everything. High-ticket offers: customs, GFE (daily texting, exclusive content, video calls, private Snap at $1K+). Create deliberate FOMO/jealousy ('you're not chatting to other OF girls, are you?'). Regular check-ins even when they haven't bought. Five to six well-managed whales can generate $20K/month on a single page.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) with going-cold detection, which is a partial match. However, there is no documented chatter-assignment protocol for whales, no response-time SLA, no free-content gifting strategy, and no GFE/high-ticket offer framework. The 'DO NOT CHAT' designation mechanic is a concrete operational step Palm lacks.
findings.recommendation: Implement a whale-designation field in the Fan Tracker CRM. Work with the external chat team to enforce a 'designated chatters only' rule for any fan spending $500+/month. Define a response-time SLA (target 30 min). Build a whale retention checklist (free content cadence, check-in frequency, high-ticket offer menu) as a build-toward document for the in-house chat team. Explore adding a 'whale offer menu' (GFE tiers, video call pricing) to the pricing SOP.

sources.quote: we have a whale who literally spends close to 10K a month on one of our models... we literally have in the name do not chat to... only two chatters are responsible
whales are fragile... if they feel ignored, if they feel undervalued or like you're just using them for money, they will leave

findings.title: Structure chat team as manager + 3 shift chatters + 1 reliever per model; pay hourly + tiered commission
findings.claim: A properly structured chat team—chat manager, three 8-hour-shift chatters, and one reliever—with a calibrated hourly/commission mix is required for 24/7 monetization at scale.
findings.what_they_do: Team per model: 1 chat manager ($300–400/week), 3 chatters on 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage, 1 reliever covering days off. Manager trains new chatters, handles whales personally, reviews performance. Chatters: $2–5/hour + 2–8% commission. On large pages (30K+/month), shift to high hourly / zero-to-1% commission because commission payouts exceed hourly cost. European chatters (Serbia, Germany, France) preferred over Philippines for quality; Philippines at $4/hour with no commission if budget-constrained. Hire from large pools: Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, OFM Telegram groups. Rule: get 500 applicants not 50; hire slow, fire fast. Monitor chatters live during first two shifts; require end-of-shift screenshot reports thereafter. Manage entire team in a structured Discord hub (clock-in/out, break channels, end-of-shift reports, chatter monitoring).
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal team structure, hiring criteria, compensation bands, or management tooling exists. This is the most detailed build-toward blueprint available for Palm's ~1yr in-house chat team roadmap. Palm has Telegram-based coordination infrastructure (§5) that could partially serve the Discord hub role.
findings.recommendation: Use this team structure as the staffing template for the in-house chat team build. Document comp bands (hourly + commission tiers by page revenue) in the pricing/commission SOP (§7 gap). Evaluate whether Palm's existing Telegram infrastructure can replace Discord for team management or whether a separate Discord hub is warranted. Begin sourcing from Upwork and OFM Telegram groups with a target pool of 100+ applicants for the first in-house chatter hire. Build the training document suite (model personality sheet, welcome SOP, fan-profile template) before the first hire.

sources.quote: chatters plus one reliever per model... each chatter works eight hour shifts... three times eight, you have full 24-hour coverage
pay for this is going to be $2 to $5 an hour... commission is going to be 2 to 8% of sales that they generate
where to find them? Upwork, online jobs.ph, OFM specific Telegram groups

findings.topic: Script flexibility vs. rigid attached-text PPVs
findings.title: Use flexible scripts with swappable content rather than fixed PPV-attached text to personalize every sale
findings.claim: Rigid PPV-attached scripts that can't be modified based on fan kinks kill conversion; chatters need script frameworks they can customize to each fan's intel profile.
findings.what_they_do: Scripts serve as structural templates (e.g. domination, soft, femdom), not word-for-word locked messages with PPVs attached. Chatters freestyle the conversation within the script structure, swapping kink references based on the fan profile gathered in intel step. If a fan's profile says 'soft content' and the chatter sends a domination framing, the fan won't buy. The model personality sheet is given to all chatters so tone and voice remain consistent while content framing varies per fan.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8); no internal script framework, chatter training documents, or model personality sheet exists. This is a direct build-toward gap for the in-house chat team and a standard Palm could push the external team to adopt now.
findings.recommendation: Create a model personality sheet for each creator (voice, tone, persona traits, hard limits) and share with the external chat team immediately. When building in-house, develop script templates per content category (soft, dominant, GFE, etc.) that are structured but kink-swappable. Prohibit PPV-attached fixed text in chatter SOPs. Make 'match script to fan intel' a core training checkpoint reviewed in first- and second-shift monitoring.

sources.quote: freestyle your text, but have scripts in place because you can change whatever you say during that script... you cannot change it if you have attached text with every single PPV
you can make a script be a domination script, a soft script, a femdom script based off text
